text: ELSENESS, a cape on the S coast of Sanday, one of the Orkney islands, in N lat. 59° 5'.
ELSEFIELD, a parish in Oxfordshire, 3 m. NE of Oxford. Area 1,200 acres. Pop. 185.
ELSENHAM, a parish in Essex, 2 m. NE of Stansted-Mount-Fichet. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 491.
ELSFLETH, a town of the grand duchy of Oldenburg, situated at the conflux of the Hunte and Weser, 18 m. NW of Bremen. Pop. 2,800. Vessels of heavy draught anchor at Brack, about 4 m. below.
ELSHAM, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. NE of Glandford-Bridge. Area 4,110 acres. Pop. 458.
ELSINBOROUGH, a township in Salem co., in the state of New Jersey, U. S., 3 m. NE of Salem. Pop. 526.
ELSING, a parish of Norfolk, 5 m. ENE of Dereham. Area 1,530 acres. Pop. 457.
ELSINORE, or ELSINEUR, in Danish, Helsin-

GOER, a well-known sea-port of Denmark, in Sieland, in the bail of Frederiksborg, on the W side of the Sound, nearly opposite to Helsingborg in Sweden, at the part where the Sound is less than 4 m. across, in N lat. 56° 2' 11", E long. 12° 36' 49", 24 m. N of Copenhagen. E. has no harbour, but an excellent roadstead, which is generally crowded with vessels entering or leaving the Baltic, and anchoring here either to pay toll, or to take in stores of some kind or other, the supply of which forms the great traffic of the place. The pop. of the town in 1846 was 8,000. Consuls of all the maritime nations of Europe are seated here, and several British mercantile establishments. No point in the navigation of the seas is more active, more concentrated, or bears heavier freight than the Sound. E. sees pass through its roadstead nearly all the trade of the Baltic. From Riga, Cronstadt, Memel, and Dantzic,-from all the Russian, Prussian, and Hanseatic ports,-are sent those immense cargoes of corn and grain, tallow, flax, hemp, and pitch, from the countries traversed by the Vistula, the Oder, the Dwina, and the fertile ELS NE coast of the island of Celebes, Asiatic archi- | countries of Esthonia, Polesia, Courland, Finland, and Livonia; and from Stockholm, Calmar, Malmoe, and Copenhagen, are despatched to S. Europe vast loads of iron, timber, and resin which form the supplies for ship-builders. All these products of the first necessity are exchanged for the manufactures of Europe, for the wines and fruits of the south, and the exotic matters and colonial productions destined for provisioning the whole basin of the Baltic-as Finland, Northern Germany, and Western Russia. The average annual number of vessels that passed the Sound, inwards and outwards, from 1827 to 1836, was 12,190; in 1844 it was 17,333; in 1849, 18,959, of which 6,885 were English. See article DENMARK, p. 877. From 1835 to 1839, the average of the ships declared at the custom-house of E. was 11,000. The dues levied by Denmark at E. are perhaps the most disgraceful of all navigation-dues. England pays them quietly, and without a murmur, though Denmark is neither historically justified in exacting them, nor does she possess the power to maintain them if England were to put in a decided remonstrance against them. The amounts levied vary from 1 to per cent. on the value of the cargoes; and according to a tariff which has not been revised during 200 years; though the value of all kinds of foreign and home produce has fallen enormously during that period, and the duty consequently amounts to 4, 5, and even 6 per cent. of their present value. Vessels passing the Sound have additional heavy expenses to pay consequent upon these duties, such as fees to officials, lighterage and porterage necessary for the examination of the cargoes, pilot-dues, and other taxes upon trade. Denmark justifies the levying of these dues on the ground of the heavy expenses she is put to by the numerous lighthouses which are necessary all along the coast; but they do not amount to one-fifth of the sums raised by the dues, and the lighthouses are equally indispensable to Danish and foreign vessels. Russia has perhaps submitted to the payment of these dues on very politic grounds. Popular reasoning attributes this submission to the desire one day or other to obtain possession of the territories on the Sound and of the dues levied therein. The ceremony of lowering in the Sound has been dispensed with in regard to all ships, both Danish and foreign, since May 1829.—On a tongue of land, a little to the NE of the town, is the fortress of Cronberg, situated on the edge of a promontory, and provided with powerful batteries, which, however, was found wholly incapable of preventing the passage of a fleet in 1801.
ELSNAPPEN, a sea-port of Sweden, in Westmannland, on the side of the Baltic, 32 m. ENE of Nyköping.
ELSON'S BAY, an inlet of the Arctic ocean to the E of Point Barrow, in N lat. 71°, W long. 156°.
ELSTEAD, a parish in Surrey, 4 m. W of Godalming. Area 4,400 acres. Pop. 743.—Also a p. in Sussex, 5 m. WSW of Midhurst. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 212.

ELSTER, the name of two rivers of Germany, one of which, called the Weisse E. or White E., rises near Asch, in the S of Saxony, holds a N course, and after passing near Leipsic, falls into the Saale, 3 m. S of Halle. Its length of course is about 110 m. Its principal affluent is the Pleisse. A considerable pearl-fishery is carried on on its banks near Oelsnits.—The Schwarz E., or Black E., rises to the S of Elster in Saxony, and, after a NW course, falls into the Elbe 6 m. above Wittemberg. Its course is about 120 m. Its principal affluent is the Röder. It was near the influx of this river that Blucher crossed the Elbe on the 3d Oct., 1813, and marched to the memorable conflicts at Leipsic.
ELSTER, a town of Saxony, in the bail. of Voitsberg, near the source of the Black Elster. Pop. 690.
ELSTERBERG, a town of Saxony, in the bail. of Plauen, on the White Elster, 7 m. N of Plauen, and 15 m. SW of Zwickau. Pop. 2,379. Woollens and cottons are manufactured here.
ELSTERWERDA, a town of Prussia, situated on the Black Elster, at the influx of the Pulsnitz, 17 m. N of Meissen, and 26 m. NNW of Dresden. Pop. 1,442.

ELSTON, or ELVESTON, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 4 m. SW of Newark. Area 1,640 acres. Pop. 285.—Also a chapelry in the p. of East Stokes, in Nottinghamshire, 3 m. SE of Southwell.—Also a township in the p. of Preston, Lancashire, 5 m. ENE of Preston.
ELSTORNWICK, a township in the p. of Humbleton, in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. ENE of Hull. Area 910 acres. Pop. 143.
ELSTOW, a parish in the co. of Bedford, 13 m. S of Bedford. Area 1,522 acres. Pop. in 1801, 475; in 1841, 562. This was the birth-place of John Bunyan, author of 'the Pilgrim's Progress.'
ELSTRA, a town of Saxony, in the circle and 12 m. W of Budissin, on the l. bank of the Black Elster. Pop. in 1834, 1,080.
ELSTREE, or IDLESTREE, a parish and v. of Hertford, 3 m. NNW of Edgeware. Area 1,370 acres. Pop. 360. Antiquaries suppose this to be the site of the Saloniacæ of Antoninus.

ELSWICK, a township in the p. of St. John, in Northumberland, 1 m. W of Newcastle. Area 720 acres. Pop. 1,789.—Also a township in the p. of St. Michael, Lancashire, 4½ m. N of Kirkham. Area 1,010 acres. Pop. 303.
ELSWORTH, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 4 m. NNE of Caxton. Area 3,700 acres. Pop. 915.
ELTEN (High and Low), two villages of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, 5 m. N of Cleves.
ELTERLEIN, a town of Saxony, in the Erzgebirge, 14 m. S of Chemnitz. Pop. 1,910.

ELTHAM, a village and parish of England, in Kent. The p. has an area of 4,230 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,627: in 1841, 2,310. The village, <math>8\frac{1}{2}</math> m. SE of London, was formerly a town of some importance. It still contains some good houses, and its environs present numerous handsome villas. The manor and manor-place were bequeathed by one of the bishops of Durham to Eleanor, queen of Edward I.; and the queen of Edward II. having been delivered of a prince here, the manor-house obtained the name of King John's palace. Festivals were celebrated here, and the place was resorted to by several succeeding sovereigns, until the reign of Henry VIII., who deserted E. for his new palace of Greenwich. The banquetting-hall, a room 100 ft. in length, and about 361 ft. in width, is still entire, and has been rescued from dilapidation by the exertions of some individuals of taste.
ELTISLEY, a parish of Cambridgeshire, 3 m. WNW of Caxton. Area 1,941 acres. Pop. 372.
ELTMANN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Lower Franconia, on the Maine, 8 m. WNW of Bamberg. Pop. 1,392.

ELTON, a township in the p. of Thornton, in Cheshire, 4 m. SW of Frodsham. Area 1,100 acres. Pop. 225.—Also a township in the p. of Warmingham, in Cheshire, 3 m. W by S of Sandbach. Area 630 acres. Pop. 570.—Also a chapelry in the p. of Youlgreave, in Derbyshire, 6 m. NW of Matlock. Area 1,210 acres. Pop. 536.—Also a parish in Durham, 3 m. SW of Stockton-upon-Tees. Area 1,060 acres. Pop. 92.—Also a p. in Herefordshire, 5 m. SW of Ludlow. Area 1,700 acres. Pop. 99.—Also a parish in Huntingdonshire, 6 m. NW of Stilton. ELV Area 3,250 acres. Pop. 844.—Also a township in the p. of Bury, in Lancashire. Area 1,990 acres. Pop. 5,202.—Also a p. in Nottinghamshire, 13 m. E by S of Nottingham. Area 980 acres. Pop. 81.

ELTON, or ALTIN-NOR, an extensive salt lake of European Russia, in the gov. of Saratof, circle of Kamyshinsk, 70 m. E of the Volga, and 200 m. S of Saratof, near the frontier of Asia. It is of an elongated form; its greatest diam., from E to W, is 13 m.; the smaller, from N to S, 10 m. Its waters are supplied by several small streams flowing from N and E, and being spread over a shallow surface estimated at 130 sq. m .- being in truth nothing more than a marsh lying above a bed of salt—the heat of the sun evaporates them, and forms the salt into a hard crust, which becomes several inches thick in the course of a season, leaving the lake in most parts dry. The pure material—a hydro-chloride of magnesia— finally obtained, amounts yearly to about 100,000 tons, and the number of workmen employed in preparing and transporting it is more than 10,000. The greater part is warehoused at Saratof.-Of all saltwaters that have been analyzed, that of Lake E. most resembles the water of the Dead sea. At a temp. of 54° its specific gravity is 1.27288.
ELTRINGHAM, a township in the p. of Ovingham, in Northumberland, 11 m. W by S of Newcastle. Pop. 87.
ELTSCH, or Jolson, a town of Hungary, in the com. and 13 m. NNW of Gömör. Pop. 4,500. It has an extensive trade in iron, which is mined in the vicinity.
ELTVIL. See ELFELD.
ELTZ, a small river of Baden, which runs into the Neckar near Mosbach. — Also another small river of Baden, which rises in the Brisgau, and runs into the Alzach near Kinzingen.

ELTZILS, a village of Turkey in Romelia, in the sanjak of Kirkkilissia, S of Adrianople. ELUCE, EL-Oos, or Zezirnalas, a town of Turkey in Asia, in the pash. and 130 m. WNW of Bagdad, and 26 m. WNW of Hit, on an island of the Euphrates, of the same name. Pop. about 2,500. The island is about a mile in length.
ELUDUMATWAL, a parish and village in the district of Tenmarachie, in the island of Ceylon. Pop. 3,000. It produces large crops of paddy.

ELUTHS, a tribe of Mongols who inhabit the district of Thsian-Hai, to the NNW of the Koko-Nor or Blue lake, bordering on the Chinese prov. of Kansu, and are also extensively scattered over Sungaria. The migrations of the pastoral nations of Asia have been so frequent, according as caprice dictated, ambition impelled, or necessity compelled, that it is not possible to unravel the web of Tartar history and geography. The first time we hear of the E., as a distinct tribe of the great Mongolian family, is about the commencement of the reign of Yong-lu, successor of Hong-vu, the founder of the Ming dynasty in China, when Ma-ha-mu, khan of the Wa-la, or Oilots, and A-lu-tai, prince of the Mongols, each received a Chinese title from the emperor Yong-lu. That of the former was Chun-ning-wang,- 'the obedient and peaceful king:' that of the latter, Honing wang,- 'the pacific and tranquil king;' so that at that period it appears the Mongols and E. were viewed as distinct hordes. whose princes condescended to receive the title of wangs-a fact which indicates the fallen fortunes of the successors of Jenghis- Khan, and that the khan of the E. branch was now independent of the Mongol khans of Kara-korom.
ELVA, a small river of Piedmont, which falls into the Sosia near Vercelli.
ELVAN, a small river of Lanarkshire, which falls into the Clyde.

ELVAS, or YELVES, a strong frontier-town of Portugal, in the prov. of Alemtejo, situated on a rocky hill not far from the r. bank of the Guadiana, and 10 m. W of the Spanish fortress of Badajoz, and 112 m. E of Lisbon. It is one of the most important strongholds in the kingdom, and has a large arsenal and manufactory of arms, spacious and bomb-proof barracks, and an immense cistern, which is supplied with water from a distance, by means of a very fine aqueduct. The outfort of La Lippe, on a hill to the N, is regarded as nearly impregnable. The town itself is old and badly built. Pop. about 16,000. It is the seat of a bishop. The adjacent country, watered by the Caia, is fruitful.
ELVASTON, a parish of Derbyshire, 4 m. SE of Derby. Area 2,760 acres. Pop. 518.
ELVEBAKEN, a village of Norway, in Finmark, near the mouth of the Alten.
ELVEDON, a parish of Suffolk, 4 m. SW of Thetford. Area 5,290 acres. Pop. 240.
ELVEN, a canton and town of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, 11 m. NE of Vannes.—The cant. comprises 6 com. Pop. in 1841, 9,391.—The com. and town had a pop. of 3,320 in 1841.
ELVEND, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Mazanderan, 40 m. SE of Fehrabad.

ELVEND, or ELWUND, the Orontes of the ancients, a branch of the Taurus, in Western Persia, separating Irak-Adjemi from Hyrcania. It is rather a group than a chain of mountains, covering, it is said, a surface of 60 sq. m. from NE to SW, and completely detached from its NW neighbours, the mountains of Ardelan. It is generally covered with snow, its summits being 41 hours' ascent above Hamadan, which is itself in a very elevated situation. The group abounds in springs of the finest water, and its lower regions are clothed with a profusion of plants.
ELVERDINGHE, a small town of Belgium, in W. Flanders, 25 m. NW of Ypres. Pop. 1,408.
ELVERLIGSEN, a village of Prussia, in Westphalia, in the circle of Altena.
ELVERUM, a town of Norway, in the bail. of Hedemarken, on the Glommen river, S of Aamodt.
ELVES (St.), a parish in Pembrokeshire, 5 m. E by S of St. David's. Pop. 34.
ELVETHAM, a parish in Hants, 5 m. NE of Odiham. Area 2,980 acres. Pop. 552.
ELVINGTON, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. ESE of York. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 478.
ELWICK, a township in the p. of Hart, co.-palatine of Durham, 9 m. NNE of Stockton-upon-Tees. Area 1,940 acres. Pop. 39.—Also a township in the p. of Belford, Northumberland, 2 m. NE of Belford.
ELWY, a river in Glamorganshire, falling into the Severn at Pennarth, after a course of 20 m.— Also a river in Denbighshire, falling into the Clwyd at St. Asaph.
ELY. See ELIE.

ELY, an ancient city, the seat of a see of the same name, in the co. of Cambridge, 72 m. NE of London, and 16 m. NNE of Cambridge, on high ground at the N extremity of the isle of E., and on the river Ouse which is navigable from Lynn to Cambridge for barges. It consists of one principal street, well-paved and lighted, with a few smaller streets branching-off in various directions, and in the centre a spacious market-place. Many of the houses have an antiquated appearance, and, except the cathedral, the town has few architectural ornaments. The town is not incorporated as a borough, and it is the only city in England which does not send members to parliament. Henry abridged the palatinate privileges of the abbots and bishops of E.; and, by 6° and 7° William IV. c. 87, their secular jurisdiction was extinguished. Potteries of coarse ware and tobacco pipes, and mills for the extraction of oils from hemp, flax, and cole seed, afford employment to a considerable number of hands. Being encompassed with fertile garden and other ground, great quantities of strawberries, cherries, greens, asparagus, &c., and dairy produce, especially butter, are sent from E. to Cambridge and London. The opening of the Eastern Counties railway branch from Lynn to E., 261 m. in length, has greatly promoted the prosperity of the city and district. The ground in the vicinity is fertile, and is celebrated for its agricultural produce. The two ps. have a united area of 17,480 acres. Pop. in 1801, 3,948; in 1831, 6,189; in 1841, 6,825. To constitute the diocese of E., in 1108, the whole of the co. of Cambridge, with the exception of a few parishes, was taken out of the jurisdiction of the see of Lincoln, and added to the isle of Ely, the abbots of which, even when the co. was part of the dio. of Lincoln, had always claimed an independent jurisdiction within the limits of their own isle. By order in council of date 19th April, 1837, and 10th April, 1839, the dio. of E. was increased by the cos. of Huntingdon and Bedford, previously in the dio. of Lincoln, and by the archd. of Sudbury, with the exception of the deaneries of Hartismere and Stow, in the co. of Suffolk, and previously in the dio. of Norwich. The total number of benefices in the dio., in 1838, was 515. The average gross yearly income of the see and of the ecclesiastical preferments, for 3 years ending 31st December, 1831, was £12,627.— The average gross yearly income of the dean and chapter of the cathedral, as a corporation aggregate. in 1831, was £8,651.—The cathedral, the workmanship of various periods, displays a singular admixture of the Saxon, Norman, and Gothic styles of architecture; yet, notwithstanding the dissimilarity of its parts, considered as a whole it is a very magnificent structure. The exterior extent of the cathedral from E to W is 535 ft.; the interior length 517 ft. The length of the transept is 190 ft.; the height of the lantern over the dome 170 ft.; the extreme height of the western tower 270 ft.; the two towers on the S wing of the latter 120 ft.; the length of the nave 203 ft.; the height of the roof over it 104 ft.; and the height of the E front to the top of the cross 112 ft. This venerable building is at present undergoing extensive repairs.—The bishop's palace is a neat brick structure.—On the N side of the cathedral is St. Mary's chapel, now Trinity church, one of the most perfect buildings of the 14th cent. Its shape is oblong; the interior length about 200 ft.; the breadth 46 ft.; and the height of the vaulted roof 60 ft.—The E. poor-law union comprehends 14 parishes, embracing an area of 128 sq. m.; with a pop. returned in 1831 at 17,327; in 1841 at 20,777. The average annual expenditure on the poor of this district, during the 3 years preceding the formation of the union, was £13,777. Expenditure in 1838, £6,125; in 1845-6, £7,257.
ELY, a small village in the p. of Llandaff, Glamorganshire, 64 m. W of Cardiff. It has a bridge across the river Elv.

ELY (ISLE OF), an extensive district of the co. of Cambridge, reaching from the bridge of Tyd on the N, to Upmere on the S, a distance of about 28 m.; and from Abbots on the E, to the river Mere near Peterborough on the W, about 25 m. On the W it is bounded by the cos. of Northampton and Huntingdon; on the N by Lincolnshire; on the E by Norfolk and part of Suffolk. This district is divided into 4 hundreds, viz.: It contains several considerable towns and vs., as Doddington, Wisbeach, Whittlesey, March, Leverington, Newton, Chatteris, &c., besides the city of E. and the vs. of Stretham and Thetford, Wilberton, Haddenham, Sutton, Mepal, Wenham, Wentworth, trict collectively makes one island, or large tract of high land encompassed with fens and formerly overflowed with water, and more strictly called the Isle of Ely. Littleport, Coveney, and Stuntney, though sometimes reckoned part of it, were in their original state disjoined by small intervals of fenny ground, and therefore were distinct islands of themselves. The more restricted tract is about 7 m. in length, and 4 m. in breadth; and is chiefly separated from the rest of the co. on the S and E by the old channel of the Ouse, which river flows from the Huntingdonshire to the Norfolk border, and washes the city of E. in its course. The face of the whole district is described by a recent writer to be "one vast plain, stretching beyond the reach of sight; interrupted on the S side by one or two ridges of comparatively high land, but in its N portion presenting only some small elevations which just lift the villages seated upon them above the general level. The whole tract is naturally a marsh, subject to be laid under water in rainy seasons by the rivers which creep through it to the sea, and rendered habitable and cultivable only by means of immense labour expended in cutting drains across it in various directions. Into these the water is raised by means of windmills, which pump it up from the ditches that everywhere intersect the low grounds; and it is retained by high banks which confine it on a level higher than that of the adjacent country." There are few parts of the country in which, perhaps, greater exertions have been made to improve and cultivate the soil than in the isle of E. Towards the close even of last century, the whole of this part of the co. was a desolate and dreary waste, almost constantly inundated by the upland waters, and perfectly destitute of anything like adequate means of drainage. Indeed, the whole surface was a series of stagnant pools, constantly producing exhalations which loaded the air with pestiferous vapours and fogs. During the last 50 years the whole appearance of this district has been changed. Mr. Marshall, the clerk to the union in the isle of E., says: "The alteration which has taken place appears to be almost the effect of magic. By the labour, industry, and spirit of the inhabitants, a forlorn waste has been converted into pleasant and fertile pastures, and the people themselves have been rewarded by bounteous harvests. Drainage, embankments, engines, and enclosures, have given stability to the soil—which in its nature is as rich as the Delta of Egypt—as well as salubrity to the air. These very considerable improvements, though carried on at a great expense, have at last turned to a double account, both in reclaiming much ground and improving the rest, and in contributing to the healthiness of the inhabitants. Works of modern refinement have given a totally different face and character to this once neglected spot. Much has been performed; much, however, yet remains to be accomplished by the rising generation. The demand for labour produced by drainage is incalculable; but when it is stated that, where sedge and rushes grew but a few years since, we now have fields of waving oats and even wheat, it must be evident that it is very great." Mr. Marshall proceeds to show the ship, Lehigh co., in the state of Pennsylvania, U.S., EMA beneficial effects which this improved system of drainage has produced upon the health of the inhathat in the decennial periods—of which 1801, 1811, and 1821 were the middle years—the baptisms and burials were as under: Years. Baptisms. Burials. Pop. in 1801. In the first of the three periods the mortality was 1 in 31; in the second, 1 in 40; in the third, 1 in 47,— the latter being less than the exact mean mortality of the kingdom for the last two years."
ELY-BAY, an indentation of the N coast of the island of Antigua, in the p. of St. George, in N lat. 17° 13′, W long. 61° 48′.

ELYRIA, a township, capital of Lorain co., in the state of Ohio, U.S., 116 m. NNE of Columbus, pleasantly situated in a level tract, enclosed by two branches of Black river, which unite half-a-mile below the village. Pop. in 1840, 1,636.
ELYTON, a village of Jefferson co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 54 m. NE of Tuscaloosa, at the head of Catoochee or Valley creek.

ELZ, a river of Baden, which takes its rise in the mountains of the Schwarzwald; passes Elzach, Waldkirch, Emmendingen, and Kenzingen; receives the Treisam; and, after a total course of 39 m., flows into the Rhine near Rust.
ELZ, or ELZBACH, a river of Baden, in the circle of the Lower Rhine, which flows into the Neckar at Neckar-Elz.

ELZA, or Elsa, a river of Tuscany, which takes its rise in the Tuscan Sub-Apennines, in the prov. and 10 m. WSW of Sienna; enters the prov. of Florence; traverses a deep valley of the same name; passes Travernelle; and after a total course, in a generally NW direction, of 42 m., unites with the Arno, 4 m. W of Empoli.

ELZACH, a market-town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, on the r. bank of the Elz, 12 m. NE of Waldkirch, at an alt. of 1,200 ft. above sealevel. Pop. 983. It possesses a considerable trade in grain.
ELZBACH. See ELSBACH.
ELZE, a town of Hanover, in the principality and 10 m. SW of Hildesheim, on the Saale, near the confluence of the Leine. Pop. 2,035. It is a royal entrepot for iron.
ELZEVIR, a township in the Victoria district of Upper Canada, to the W of Kaladar township.

EMADU, a town of Dahomey, in SW. Africa, 17 m. N of Jenna. The approach to it from the S is described by Clapperton as very beautiful, being through a long and broad avenue of trees, at the end of which a stockade 18 ft. high, with a wicker gate, and another of the same kind at the distance of 100 paces, defend the entrance of the town. The surface of the adjoining country appears broken into gentle hills and dales, with a small stream running through every valley.

EMANUEL, a county of the state of Georgia, U. S., comprising an area of 1,100 sq. m., bordered on the N by Ogeechee river, and watered by Cannouchee and Ohoopee rivers, and their branches. It is generally level, and its soil is sandy and destitute of fertility. Pop. in 1840, 3,129. Its capital is Swainsboro.
EMATRIS, a parish of co. Monaghan, 31 m. W of Ballybay. Area 12,298 acres. Pop. 7,643.
EMAUS, a Moravian village in Salisbury town- 87 m. NE of Harrisburg, at the foot of South mountain. It consisted in 1840 of a single street of about 25 houses.

EMBA, or JEM, a river of Independent Tartary, which takes its rise in the Urkach or Camel mountains, in the steppe of the Little Kirghiz; runs SW between the Bosaga-Tau and Kara-Tagh; thence takes a WSW direction along the S side of the Balkum-Tagh, and through the steppe of Embin or Mamai, to the Caspian, which it enters at a bay of the same name, after a total and rapid course of 300 m. Its breadth varies from 120 to 240 ft. Its waters in the upper part of its course are extremely muddy, but towards its mouth it abounds with fish. Its principal affluent is the Uzuntemir or Sogatemir river, which it receives on the r. E. bay forms the NE extremity of the Caspian sea to the SE of the mouths of the Ural. Its embrasure is 90 m. in depth, and about 105 at its greatest breadth, and is thickly studded with islands.—The fisheries of the E., comprising 300 m. of the Caspian coast, from the mouth of the Ural to Mentovi-Kultuk, have been free since 1803.

EMBABEH, a village of Lower Egypt, in the prov. of Ghizeh, on the l. bank of the Nile, opposite Bulak, and about 3 m. NW of Cairo. Its environs are noted for their produce of excellent butter. The defeat of the Mamalukes by Napoleon, in 1798, took place in this locality.
EMBACA. See AMBACCA.

EMBACH, a river of Russia in Europe, in the gov. of Livonia, which issues from a small lake in N lat. 58°, runs SW, bends W, then N, and passes through Lake Virtzerv, issuing at the NE extremity, directs its course to the E past Derpt, or Dorpat, and falls into Lake Peipous. The upper and lower portions of this river, 36 and 42 m. respectively in length, are named the Little and Great Embach. It has a total course of 78 m., and is navigable to a considerable distance above Dorpat. Its principal affluent is the Aoua.

EMBALIRE, or Believe, a river which has its source in the Pyrenees, in the valley and 13 m. NE of Andorra, receives the Ordina, enters the Spanish prov. of Lerida, and, after a total course of 27 m. in a generally SSW direction, flows into the r. bank of the Segre, 2 m. S of Urgel.
EMBDEN. See EMDEN.
EMBEL, a mountain of France, in the dep. of the Drôme, cant. and 21 m. E of Valence.
EMBER AND WESTON, a hamlet in the p. of Thames-Ditton, Surrey. Area 2,900 acres. Pop. 1,256.
EMBERTON, or EMMERTON, a parish of Buckinghamshire, 13 m. S of Olney, and SE of the Ouse. Area 1,860 acres. Pop. 658.
EMBID-DE-LA-RIBERA, a town of Spain, in Aragon, prov. of Zaragoza, partido and 16 m. W of Calatayud, on the l. bank of the Deza.
EMBIES (ISLE DES), a fortified island of France, in the Mediterranean, 9 m. SW of Toulon. It is 15 m. in length, and about a mile in breadth.

EMBIN, or Mama'ı (Steppe of), an extensive district of Independent Tartary, stretching E from the Caspian, along the S bank of the Emba river, to the range of the Uch-kanat-Tau-by which it is separated from the district of Great Barsuk-and skirted on the S by a range of mountains terminating in the E in Mount Túmán. It comprises an area about 300 m. in length from E to W, and 66 m. in breadth; on the W, extending along the shore of the Caspian, are the Sand-hills of Little Airák-li, and lakes in great numbers stud the greater part of its surface. Its inhabitants consist chiefly of the migratory tribes of the Kirgiz-kaizáks.

EMBITU'BA, a small maritime town of Brazil, EMB in the prov. of Santa-Catharina, in the bay and near the cape of the same name, in the parish and 18 m. NE of Santa-Anna-de-Villa-Nova. The bay is sheltered from the S and SW winds, and is capable of receiving vessels of considerable size.
EMBLEHEM, a village of Belgium, in the prov. and 10 m. SE of Antwerp. Pop. 764.

EMBLETON, a chapelry in the p. of Brigham, Cumberland, 23 m. SE of Cockermouth and S of the Derwent. Pop. 408.—Also a township in the p. of Sedgefield, co. of Durham, 7 m. ESE of Hartlepool. Area 3,160 acres. Pop. 98.—Also a parish and township of Northumberland, 61 m. NNE of Alnwick.
EMBLICHEIM, a village and castle of Hanover. in the co. and 27 m. NNW of Bentheim, bail. of Nauenhaus, on the r. bank of the Vechte. Pop. 808.

EMBOMMA, a town of Lower Guinea, 150 m. SE of Loanga, and 75 m. WNW of San Salvador, on the r. bank of the Zaire. Pop. 500. It is an entrepot for European merchandise for the inland trade, and was formerly the most important slavemarket frequented by the Portuguese.
EMBORROW, a parish of Somerset, 5 m. NE of Wells. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. 210.

EMBOTETIU' MONDEGO, or ARANIANI, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto-Grosso, which descends from the Serra-Galhano, unites with the Verde and Zezere, passes the fortified town of Miranda, and falls into the Paraguay, 30 m. below the confluence of the Taquari, or Tacoary. It is navigable in its entire extent.
EMBOURG, a commune of Belgium, in the prov. of Liege, 7 m. SW of Fleron. Pop. 661. It has extensive iron-founderies.

EMBRACH, a bailiwick, parish, and village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 9 m. NNE of Zurich, in a fertile valley. Pop. 1,216. The village has a handsome church. The bail, contains 14 parishes and 15,450 inhabitants. Straw-work forms the chief branch of local industry.
EMBRO, a village of Upper Canada, in the W of the township of Zorra. Pop. about 150, chiefly from the Highlands of Scotland.

EMBRUN, an arrondissement, canton, commune, and town of France, in the dep. of Hautes-Alpes. The arrond. comprises an area of 143,739 hect., and the 5 cant. of Chorges, Embrun, Guillestre, Orcieres, and Savines. Pop. in 1831, 30,828; in 1846, 32.402. The cant. contains 8 com. Pop. in 1831, 10,146; in 1841, 11,414.—The town is situated on a rocky plateau, which rises in the midst of an extensive plain, and at the foot of which flows the Durance, at an alt. of 2,145 ft. above sea-level, 21 m. E of Gap. Pop. in 1789, 2,678; in 1831, 3,062; in 1841, 4,373. It is well-built and fortified, and is intersected in its entire extent by a handsome street. The principal buildings are the cathedral—a fine edifice generally attributed to Charlemagne—the episcopal palace, the college, and the barracks. Cloth, hats, tape, and silk cord, form the chief articles of manufacture. Its trade chiefly consists in sheep, leather, hats, and provisions. A fair is held once a-year. Marble is quarried in the environs.—This town—the Ebrodunum of the Romans—is of great antiquity. Its foundation is attributed to Allobrox, a Gallic prince. It formed, in the time of Adrian, the metropolis of the Maritime Alps, and more recently the capital of the French district of Embrunois. In 1573, it was taken and pillaged by the free bands; and in 1692, it met with a similar fate at the hands of the Duke of Savoy. It has been the seat of several councils, the last of which was held in 1727. Its ancient bishopric was suppressed at the revolution.
EMBSAY WITH EASTBY, a township in the p. and 2 m. ENE of Skipton, W. R. of Yorkshire, near the Leeds and Liverpool canal. Area 3,960 acres. Pop. 962.
EMDEHLY, a small town and fortress of Hindostan, in the prov. of Oude, 18 m. SE of Lucknow, near the r. bank of the Gumti.

EMDEN, or EMBDEN, a bailiwick and town of Hanover, in the landr. of Aurich, principality of E. Friesland.—The bail. has an area of 78 sq. m.—The town is situated in N lat. 53° 22', E long. 7° 12', near the efflux of the Ems into the Dollart-bay. It is a fine old town, and the chief commercial place in Hanover. It greatly resembles a Dutch town, and is intersected by numerous canals. It has 6 churches, a synagogue, a gymnasium, a school-of-navigation, and a public library of 3,000 vols. There are manufactories of cottons, stockings, sail-cloth, soap, leather, and tobacco, and numerous breweries and distilleries; and the town enjoys a considerable commerce in grain, butter, cheese, and linens. The herring-fishery conducted from this port is said to employ from 1,200 to 1,500 hands. A canal 2 m. in length connects the port with the river Ems. The pop. of the town in 1846 was 12,300; in 1652, it is said to have amounted to 20,000. It has been a free port since 1751.

EM-DOKNE, a village of Darfur, 100 m. SW of Kobbe. EMEGRANTE GRENSGEBIET [i. e. 'Emigrant's Territory'], a name applied by emigrant Dutch boers to the whole of the country E of the Great Desert, and N of the Orange river sovereignty and the settlement of Natal, lying between the parallels of 20° and 28° S. The following notice of this region is abridged from a recent article in the Daily News. Since the emigration of the boers beyond the frontier-line of the British settled territories began, as noticed in the article CAPE OF GOOD HOPE, these dissatisfied colonists have been gradually penetrating further into the interior of Africa, in a NE direction. They first crossed the Orange river, and spread themselves over the plains in the triangle enclosed by its upper course, its great affluent, the Vaal river, and the Quathlamba or Drakenberg mountains. Having discovered passes into Natal, nearly midway between the sources of the Vaal and the Orange rivers, they next came down upon the coast and formed settlements at Pietermaritzburg and Congella. Upon the annexation of Natal to the British territory, the more irreconcileable of the boers again withdrew to the E of the mountains; and upon the annexation of the Orange River sovereignty, they withdrew to the N of the Vaal. Even before these events considerable numbers of them had penetrated far to the northward. The Emegrante Grensgebiet is the name applied vaguely by the boers to the whole of the country E of the Great Desert. In so far, however, as we have yet been able to ascertain, it is only a broad central section of this region in which the boers are to be found. Their settlements do not extend to the Great Desert on the W, or to the sea-coast on the E. How far they extend to the N is probably imperfectly known, even to themselves. To the N of Graham's town, and E of Cradock, on the E frontier of the Cape colony, a mountain-range of considerable elevation begins, which extends in a NE direction, separating the waters of the Orange river basin, from those that flow through Kaffirland, Natal, and the countries further NE. The most northerly tributaries of the Vaal rise in this range a little to the N of 27° S lat., and a little to the W of 30° E long. About 26° S lat. there is another water-shed, extending W from this mountain-range towards the Great Desert, and separating the affluents of the Orange river, and the waters which flow towards and are lost in the Desert, Mexico in this respect, that its great height above * EME from the waters of the Limpopo. The principal rivers that form by their junction what is called the Limpopo, are the Notuani on the W, the Meriqua in the middle, and the Ori, Oori, or Oli, on the E. The junction of these three rivers takes place about half a degree to the S of the tropic of Capricorn, and nearly in 28° E long. From that point, the downward course of the Limpopo has been traced to about 22° 20' S lat., and 29° 20' E long., where the river turns due E. Of its further progress we have no accounts from European eye-witnesses; but the best information, collected from native sources, seems to render it certain, that after flowing about 70 or 80 m. to the E, the Limpopo turns round to the S and SE and falls into Delagoa bay. In the early Portuguese maps of Africa, a great inland lake was placed somewhere about 20° S lat. and 23° E long. It was first omitted by D'Anville, who could obtain no positive evidence of its existence. Of late years, however, information collected by missionaries from the natives revived the belief that the lake really existed, and nearly in the situation assigned to it by the Portuguese. After various fruitless attempts to reach it, this lake was re-discovered in July, 1849, by the Rev. Mr. Livingstone, accompanied by Messrs. Oswell and Murray. According to the observations of these gentlemen, it is situated immediately to the S of 20° S lat., and extends from the 23d to the 24th meridian of E long.; and is elevated 2,825 ft. above the level of the sea. From its E extremity issues a large river called the Zouga, along the banks of which Mr. Livingstone and his companions travelled 300 m. Its downward course from the lake is for the first 150 m. nearly due E.; for the other 150 it serpentines in a general direction to the SSE. Mr. Arrowsmith infers, with great probability, that the Zouga joins the Limpopo somewhere below the point at which that river turns off to the E.—The upper sources of the Notuani are in the undisturbed possession of Bechuana tribes, who appear to extend thence S to the Vaal, and E and NE to the Great Desert and Lake Ngami. To the SE of these tribes, where the Vaal joins the Orange river, are Griqua settlements. Along the coast, from the NE boundary of Natal to Delagoa bay, we find no traces of the boers; but they are found on all the affluents which join the Vaal, from the N as far W as 27° E long.; on both sides of the crest of both the watersheds described above; on both sides of the Limpopo as far as we have accounts of it from eye-witnesses; in the direction of the Zouga, ten or twelve days' journey to the north of the great eastern bend of the Limpopo; and on what we have reason to believe is the lower course of the Limpopo, about 130 m. to the NE of Delagoa bay.—The Emegrante Grensgebiet enjoys a temperature more moderate than its partly tropical position might lead people to expect. The range of mountains extending from SW to NE, within Cape colony, attains an elevation of 7,000 ft., and snow lies on them part of the year. To the N of the passes into Natal the height of the range appears to diminish, but it is still considerable. The mountain-chain which runs from E to W between Potschifstrom and Magaliesberg attains in many places an elevation of not less than 5,000 ft. The elevation of the great lake is estimated at nearly 3,000 ft. It is apparent from the courses of the river that the country between that lake and Magaliesberg must be still higher. The character of the Zouga and Limpopo rivers indicates a very gradual declivity in the valleys of both; and the mountains which run along the r. bank of the latter have evidently a considerable altitude. The Emegrante Grensgebiet has a strong analogy with Central the sea adapts it for the habitation of Europeans notwithstanding its partially tropical position. The configuration of the ground in this region, except at one part, resembles much the mountainous country immediately to the N and E of Graham's Town. There are the same deep ravines and abrupt acclivities; the same intermixture of good pastures, and spots susceptible of tillage with wide barren patches. There is in the atmosphere the same tendency to dryness which makes S. Africa so healthy; with less frequent droughts owing to the greater part of the country being situated in those latitudes where the year is divided into a wet and dry season. In the ravines and along the water-courses wood is abundant, and attains a good size. The abundance of game-bocks, elands, elephants, rhinoceroses, lions, &c.—indicates good pasturage; and the rapidity with which the herds of the boers have increased shows that the indication is not deceptive. It is a favoured country for the hunter, and the trader in ivory, ostrich feathers, and other native produce, and for the herdsman. There is one part of this region an exception to the above account. In the angle of the Great desert which runs in between Lake Ngami and the Zouga river on the N, and the Limpopo on the SE, water fails on the surface, and salt marshes dried up in the dry season abound. But even this district is intersected with shallow depressions, called 'sand-rivers' by the boers, where water is always to be found by digging. Our information is too meagre to enable us to give precise accounts of the civil government which the boers have organised among themselves. They have a landdrost and heemraad at the settlement on the Mooi river, and probably at all their settlements. Pretorius, in his manifestoes, designates himself 'Commandant-general,' and they talk of their 'republic.' It is obvious, however, that each settlement is in fact a little independent democracy, where the will of the majority is law. The greater part of the boers are farmers and labourers from the back-settlements, or their descendants. To their credit be it said, they have retained a reverential sentiment in matters of religion. Every man among them rides, and rides well, and uses his gun with skilll. Their aversion to the British government is extreme; in their dealings with the natives, without being absolutely cruel, they are apt to be overbearing and despotic.
EMENABAD, or Eminabad, a village of the Punjaub, in the prov. and 35 m. NNW of Lahore.

EMERALD ISLAND, or MACOUR, an island in the Red sea, off the Egyptian coast, in N lat. 23° 50', and E long. 36° 52′ 30″, 3½ m. SE of Ras-Benass. It is not above 1 m. in length, and rises in the centre to an alt. of about 100 ft.
EMERIAU (Point), a headland on the coast of W. Australia, in S lat. 16° 46'. It is formed of tall white cliffs springing from a base of black rocks.

EMERINA, a province of Madagascar, which may be regarded as the central portion of the island, but whose geographical position is not correctly known. It is the most elevated district in the island, and, for the same reason, also the healthiest. The inhabitants of this district of Madagascar call themselves Hawa, or Ambaniandru. In person, they are about the middling stature of Europeans; their colour varies considerably, some being very black, others swarthy, but the greater number of an olive brown complexion. All the blacks have woolly hair, like the Negroes of the African coast; while those resembling mulattoes, or Indians, have long hair like Europeans. Their features are regular, with fine eyes, and wellset teeth, which they blacken at intervals with the root of the lingun, for the purpose of rendering them whiter. In disposition they are said to be lively and | EMI obliging; but vain, capricious, revengeful, superstitious, and avaricious. They are ready in the use and application of their bodily powers; and in the khabars or great assemblies often exhibit much genius and natural eloquence.—The prov. of E. is subdivided into several districts and dependencies.—Rice, the great object of Madagascar culture, and the principal article of food to the inhabitants, is cultivated on the low lying grounds, where the water does not run off, or the sides of the rivers, where inundation is easy. After having divided a plot into little squares, the rice is thrown in. It soon germinates, and, after transplantation, yields a hundred-fold. After rice, manioc and batatas are the chief articles of food. The roots of manioc often here acquire a monstrous size. Messrs. Hilsenberg and Bojer, two German botanists, say they have measured some which were 15 ft. long, and almost a foot in diam. Then come maize, seasoned herbs, 'giromonds,' calabashes, earth nuts, sugar-canes, pine apples, breadfruit, the vine, and potatoes.—Among the articles of manufacture are cotton and hemp.—E. is productive in cattle, which are remarkably large and fat. The sheep differ little from goats, being covered with hair instead of wool; their heads, however, are broader, and their tails so big as frequently to weigh 9 or 10 lbs. Hogs and cabris likewise abound; but by an absurd command of the ancient kings, they are not allowed to come near the cap., and must always be kept at 5 or 6 leagues' distance.
EMERSBURG, a village of Austria, in the circle of the Weiner Walde, W of Neustadt.
EMERY (Point), the E cliffy head of Port Darwin, on the N coast of Australia, in S lat. 12° 27', E long. of Port Essington 1° 19'.
EMERINGES, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Rhone, cant. of Beaujeu. Pop. 339. It affords good wine.

EMERY, a river of the state of Tennessee, U. S., formed by the junction of several streams, which descend from the Cumberland mountains, in Morgan co. It runs first NE and then SE, between Anderson and Roane counties; and after a total course of 33 m., unites with the Clinch.
EMETZ, a river of Russia in Asia, in the gov. of Tobolsk, which unites with the Vagaï, an affluent of the Ichim, at Malishensk, after a course to the E of about 60 m.

EMFRAS, a town of Abyssinia, in the kingdom of Amhara, in the district of Belessen, 25 m. S of Gondar, on a height on the l. bank of the Arno, an affluent of Lake Dembea. Pop. about 1,500. A considerable trade is carried on in cloves and civet.
EMIKASSOS, a river of Turkey in Europe, in the prov. of Thessaly, which has its source in Mount Hellovo, runs NE, and in union with the Gura falls into the Sataldge.
EMIL, or EMIR, a river of Asia, which takes its rise in Sungaria, and falls into the E side of the Kourghe-Nor, in the S part of the Russian gov. of Omsk.
EMILIA (Point), a low tongue of land on the W coast of Sicily, between Trapani and Cape Cofano.
EMILION (SAINT), a commune of France, in the dep. of the Gironde, cant. and 5 m. ESE of Libourne. Pop. 3,000. It is noted for its wine.
EMILY, a township of Upper Canada, in the Colborne district. It is generally fertile and well-cultivated. Its inhabitants are chiefly Irish Protestants.

EMINEH (CAPE), a headland of Turkey in Europe, on the Black sea, on the confines of Bulgaria and Rumelia, in N lat. 42° 41′ 40″, and E long. 27° 10', forming the E extremity of the Emineh Dagh or Balkan mountains, and hence called by the ancients Hami Extrema. See BALKAN.
EMIRKOI, a town of Turkey in Europe, in the sanj. of Silistria, and SW of Hadji Oglu Bazarjik.

EMJESSEN, a caravanserai and halting-place in the Great desert, on the route to Ghadames from Tripoli, 20 m. NE of Ghadames. The environs present a vast salt plain, covered in different parts with a hard thick coating of salt; the encircling Sahara is utterly sterile and barren.
EMLAGH, a parish in co. Meath, 3 m. NE of Kells. Area 2,119 acres. Pop. 426.
EMLEBEN, a village of Saxe-Coburg-Gotha, 5 m. S of Gotha, on the Leine. Pop. 671.
EMLEY, a parish and township in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. ESE of Huddersfield. Area 3,120 acres. Pop. 2,568; of township 1,575.

EMLY, a parish, containing a small town of the same name, in co. Tipperary. Area 9,183 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,838; in 1841, 4,011.—The small market and episcopal town of E. stands 63 m. WSW of Tipperary, on the road to Charleville. Though now a mere v., it is noticed by some ancient historians as in their day a large and flourishing city. Pop. in 1831, 701; in 1841, 650.—The sees of E. and Cashel were united in 1568; and both were united by the act of 1833 to the sees of Waterford and Lismore. See Cashel. The length of the dio. is 41 m.; its breadth 15 m.; its area 186,529 acres. Pop. in 1831, 90.969. It comprises considerable portions of the W side of co. Tipperary, and the E side of co. Limerick.
EMLY, a small town of Hindostan, in the prov. and 95 m. NE of Delhi, and 15 m. W of Hurdwar, on an affluent of the Ganges.
EMLYFADD, a parish of co. Sligo. Area 9,453 acres. Pop. 4,811. It contains the town of Ballymote, 12 m. N of Boyle.
EMLYGRENNAN, a parish of co. Limerick. Area 2,513 acres. Pop. 1,387.
EMLYN, a hamlet in the p. of Kenarth, Carmarsmell. thenshire. Pop. 1,049.

EMMELOORT, a village of Holland, in the N part of the island of Schockland, in the Zuyder-Zee, 10 m. E of Vollenhove. EMMEN, EMME, or GROSS-EMMEN, a river of Switzerland, rising near Brienz, in the cant. of Berne, and flowing into the Aar, on the r. bank, at Emmenholze, after a course of 42 m.
EMMEN, or Klein-Emmen, a river of Switzerland, rising near the source of the Grosse-Emmen, and flowing through the valley of Entlebach to the Reuss, which it joins on the l. bank, near Lucerne.
EMMENDINGEN, a town of Baden, in the circle of the Upper Rhine, on the Brettenbach, 19 m. SSE of Strasburg. Pop. 2,000. It was formerly the cap. of the margraviate of Hachberg.

EMMER, two small rivers of Prussian Westphalia, the larger of which, rising near Driburg, falls into the Weser, 3 m. S of Hameln, after a course of 28 m. The Little E. empties itself into the Werse, near the town of Munster.
EMMERES, a large village of Holland, in the prov. of Utrecht, 7 m. NW of Amersfoort.

EMMERICH, a town of Rhenish Prussia, in the Rees circle, on the r. bank of the Rhine, 5 m. NE of Cleves. Pop. 6,380. It is a place of active trade and importation; and has regular steam-boat communication with Strasburg and Deventer.
EMMERIN, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Habourdin. Pop. 1,161.
EMMERLEV, a village of Denmark, in Sleswick, 2 m. N of Hoven.
EMMERSDORF, a town of Lower Austria, on the Danube, 15 m. SW of Krems. Pop. 250.
EMMETT, a township in Calhoun co., in Michigan, U. S. Pop. 647.

EMMETTSBURG, a village in Frederick co., in - EMU the state of Maryland, U. S., 50 m. NW of Baltimore.—Mount St. Mary's Roman Catholic college is 2 m. distant from the v. It was founded in 1830, and has 17 professors.
EMNABAD, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Dowlatabad, 18 m. SW of Beder.
EMOULIE'RE, a commune of France, in the dep. of Haute-Saone, cant. of Faucogney. Pop. 1,286.
EMOY. See AMOY.

EMPOLI, a town of Tuscany, in the prov. and 16 m. W by S of Florence, on the l. bank of the Arno. Pop. 5,550. It has manufactories of cotton, leather, and straw-bonnets. The v. of Empoli Vecchio or Old E. is in the neighbourhood.
EMPONGO, a town of Mocaranga, in E. Africa, on the Zambese, in N lat. 16°.
EMPOSTA. See AMPOSTA.
EMPTINNE, a village of Belgium, in the prov. of Namur, 3 m. NNE of Ciney. Pop. 330.

EMS, a river of Prussian Westphalia, which rises in the Teutsburger-wald, in the principality of Paderborn; flows W and then N, through the principalities of Munster and E. Friesland; and discharges itself by two arms into Dollart bay in the North sea, at Petkum, a little below Emden, after a course of 250 m., in which it passes Rietberg, Talgte, Rheina, Lingen, Meppen, and Leer. Its breadth at the mouth is considerable; and the tide rises in it for a number of miles, rendering it an important medium of transport into the interior. Its principal affluents are the Werse, the Aa, the Haase, and the Leda. It is navigable from Le Rheina downwards. EMS, BAD-EMS, or DORF-EMS, a town of Nassau, on the r. bank of the Lahn, 8 m. ESE of Coblenz. Pop. 1,800. It has celebrated mineral springs, of a temp. of from 75° to 132°. They yield in 24 hours 12,400 cubic ft. of water, of a faintly sulphureous
EMSBACH, a river of Germany, which runs into the Lahn, 3 m. E of Limburg.
EMSBUHREN, a village of Hanover, 10 m. S by W of Lingen, near the l. bank of the Ems. Pop. 309.
EMSKIRCHEN, a town of Bavaria, in the circle of Middle Franconia, 9 m. E of Neustadt, on the Aurach. Pop. 753.
EMSTECK, a village of Oldenburg, 5 m. SW of Alhorn. Pop. 535.

EMSTER, or Embscher, a river of Prussian Westphalia, rising on the N flank of the Haardstrangberge, and flowing through the circles of Arnsberg and Munster to the Rhine, into which it falls on the r. bank, a little to the N of Ruhrort.
EMSWELL, a township in the p. of Driffield, E. R. of Yorkshire. Area, with Kelleythorpe, 2,100 acres. Pop. 100.
